title: Hierarchical reinforcement learning with dynamic recurrent mechanism for course recommendation

text.content: Hierarchical reinforcement learning with dynamic recurrent mechanism for course recommendation

title.plain_text: Hierarchical reinforcement learning with dynamic recurrent mechanism for course recommendation

tables.heading: Summary Table REC
rows:
- User initial states are computed from learner profile vectors (historical learning behavior, interests and knowledge coverage).

- Reward scaling is initialized as 1

Q-network weights are randomly initialized for both agents

- High-level agent selects a learning goal.

- Low-level agent recommends a course sequence to achieve the goal.

- Rewards are dynamically adjusted on learner’s engagement, completion and satiscfction after each interaction.

- Policies are updated using a Dynamic Recurrent Mechanism

- Inactive users were removed, leaving in total 32000 students.

Course vectors were created using Word2Vec

Learner features are normalized and concatenated with engagement metrics

Authors use Holdout Method, making the separation by Learner ID.

Authors use Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning to develop a dual-based recommender system:

Higher-level Agent: Decides the students’ learning goal, 

Lower-level Agent: Recommends Specific Courses

This system outperforms multiple baselines

Authors create 2 alternative HELARs:

HELAR_W → brings the Attention Coefficient into the objective function

HELAR_A → Brings Attention Weight Matrix into Objective Function

- Model training requires enough data for 2 Q-matrices

- Reward design depends on platform-specific metrics

- No specific handling of cold-start scenario

blocks.text: The authors adddress the course selection in an online scenario problem by using Reinforcement Learning to create a dynamic representation of learners. However RL approaches often come short due to the exploration-exploitation tradeoff. Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning with Dynamic Recurrent mechanism (HELAR) includes a novel policy gradient method → a recurrent scheme that exploits current knowledge while also exploring learning preferences.

blocks.text: Instead of recommending a single next course, this system builds a two-level decision process:

blocks.text: The upper agent decides what kind of learning goal (topic area or difficulty level) a student should pursue next.

text.content: (topic area or difficulty level) a student should pursue next.

rich_text.plain_text: (topic area or difficulty level) a student should pursue next.

blocks.text: The lower agent then recommends specific courses to achieve that goal.
It learns from historical behavior and continuously updates its strategy as learners engage (or disengage) with the platform.

text.content: It learns from historical behavior and continuously updates its strategy as learners engage (or disengage) with the platform.

rich_text.plain_text: It learns from historical behavior and continuously updates its strategy as learners engage (or disengage) with the platform.

blocks.text: Traditional recommenders (like CF or GRU4Rec) only look at short-term clicks. Here, reinforcement learning (RL) focuses on long-term learning success—whether a student completes and benefits from a sequence of courses. The “dynamic reward adjustment” gives higher feedback when engagement increases and penalizes the agent when students drop out or rate courses poorly.

blocks.text: The HRL model achieves around 10 % higher precision and recall than strong deep-learning baselines.
The dynamic reward makes it more adaptive—it notices when a learner’s motivation wanes and shifts recommendations accordingly.

blocks.text: This hierarchical setup models realistic learning goals better than single-step recommenders.

text.content: This hierarchical setup models realistic learning goals better than single-step recommenders.

rich_text.plain_text: This hierarchical setup models realistic learning goals better than single-step recommenders.

blocks.text: The system learns to recommend whole learning pathways instead of isolated courses, balancing exploration (new topics) and exploitation (familiar domains). It’s powerful but computationally demanding, requiring careful reward tuning and large-scale behavioral data.
